title: Encapsulation and Access Modifiers
description: Hiding a class's internal fields behind private and controlled getter/setter methods, plus the four access modifier levels.
objectives: Understand encapsulation as bundling data with controlled access
Use private fields with public getter and setter methods
Know all four Java access modifiers and when each applies

sections.text: In Classes and Objects, we wrote s1.age = 21; directly. But nothing stops someone from also writing s1.age = -50;, which makes no sense for a person's age. Encapsulation is how we prevent this.

Bundling an object's data (fields) together with the methods that operate on it, while restricting direct outside access to that data — typically by making fields private and exposing controlled access through public methods.
Once name and age are private, code OUTSIDE this class (like Main) can no longer write s1.name or s1.age at all — that line would fail to compile. We need another way in: getters and setters.
A public method (conventionally named getFieldName) that returns the current value of a private field.
A public method (conventionally named setFieldName) that updates a private field's value, optionally validating the new value first.

setAge(-5) silently does nothing here, since -5 fails the newAge >= 0 check — this is the entire benefit of encapsulation: setAge decides what counts as a valid age, something a plain public field could never enforce.

sections.title: Access modifiers, from most to least restrictive
rows: Same class and any class in the same package (packages covered soon)
Same package, plus subclasses in other packages (subclasses covered in Inheritance)

sections.text: Writing no modifier at all is itself a real, distinct access level (package-private) — it is not automatically public. We fully use public and private in this course for now; protected and package-private become relevant once Inheritance and Packages are covered.

sections.title: Benefits
points: Validation — a setter can reject invalid values (like a negative age) that a plain public field could never stop
Flexibility — the internal representation can change later without breaking outside code, as long as the getter/setter behavior stays the same
Read-only fields — provide only a getter, no setter, to make a field visible but not directly changeable from outside

sections.title: Why this design matters
points: balance is private -- no outside code can set it directly to an arbitrary value
deposit() and withdraw() are the only ways to change it, and both validate the amount
withdraw(1000) is silently rejected since it exceeds the current balance -- an invalid state is prevented entirely

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Making fields private but forgetting to write any getters/setters at all, making the field completely inaccessible from outside.
Writing a setter with no validation logic, defeating the entire purpose of encapsulation -- it becomes no safer than a public field.
Assuming no access modifier means public -- it actually means package-private, a distinct, more restrictive level.
Making everything public out of convenience, skipping encapsulation entirely, even for fields that genuinely need validation or protection.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is encapsulation?
questions.answer: Bundling an object's data together with the methods that operate on it, while restricting direct outside access to that data -- typically by making fields private and exposing controlled access through public getter and setter methods.

questions.question: What are the four access modifiers in Java, from most to least restrictive?
questions.answer: private (same class only), package-private / no modifier (same package), protected (same package plus subclasses elsewhere), and public (accessible from anywhere).

questions.question: Why use a setter instead of making a field public?
questions.answer: A setter can validate incoming values before applying them, rejecting invalid states (like a negative age or an over-limit withdrawal) that a plain public field could never prevent.

questions.question: How would you make a field readable from outside a class but not directly modifiable?
questions.answer: Make the field private and provide only a public getter for it, with no corresponding setter.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Encapsulation bundles an object's data with the methods that operate on it, hiding fields as private and exposing controlled access through public getter and setter methods. This lets a setter validate incoming values -- rejecting a negative age or an over-limit withdrawal -- something a plain public field could never enforce. Java has four access levels, from most to least restrictive: private (same class), package-private/no modifier (same package), protected (same package plus subclasses), and public (everywhere). Providing only a getter with no setter creates an effectively read-only field from the outside.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: Encapsulation = a locked box (private field) with a controlled slot (public getter/setter). The slot decides what's allowed in or out -- nobody reaches directly into the box.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: What is encapsulation, in your own words?
List Java's four access modifiers from most to least restrictive.
Why is a setter better than a public field when validation matters?
How do you make a field readable but not writable from outside its class?
